title: Butter Chicken (or Paneer / Tofu)
description: A rich North Indian butter chicken inspired by old Delhi restaurants with a slow-cooked tomato-onion gravy, aromatic whole spices, butter, cream, and smoky tandoori chicken. Paneer or tofu can be substituted for a vegetarian version.

ingredients.note: use tandoori chicken; paneer or tofu can be substituted
optional; add only at the end
optional, only if tomatoes are too acidic

steps: Prepare the protein first. Traditionally, butter chicken is made with cooked tandoori chicken. Grill or roast marinated chicken thighs until lightly charred and almost cooked through. For a vegetarian version, lightly pan-fry paneer or extra-firm tofu until golden.
Heat the oil and half of the butter in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat.
Add the whole spices: green cardamom, cloves, bay leaves, and cinnamon. Cook for about one minute until fragrant.
Add the garlic and ginger. Sauté for 1–2 minutes until the raw aroma disappears.

Add the chopped onions with a generous pinch of salt. Cook slowly for 20–30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they become deep golden brown. Do not rush this step—it develops much of the final flavor.
Add the Kashmiri red chilli powder and stir for 30 seconds. Avoid burning the spices.
Add the crushed tomatoes and mix thoroughly. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook uncovered for 30–40 minutes until the tomatoes become sweet, thick, and the butter begins separating around the edges.
Remove the bay leaves, cinnamon stick, and as many whole cloves and cardamom pods as possible.

Using an immersion blender (or carefully transferring to a countertop blender), blend the gravy until completely smooth and silky. For a restaurant-style finish, strain through a fine mesh sieve if desired.
Return the smooth gravy to the pot. Stir in the remaining butter and heavy cream until fully incorporated.

Add the grilled tandoori chicken (or paneer/tofu) and simmer over very low heat for at least 60 minutes. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking. The slow simmer allows the smoky protein to absorb the buttery tomato gravy and develops the characteristic Delhi restaurant flavor.
Taste and adjust the seasoning. If the tomatoes are still slightly acidic, add a small amount of honey or sugar.
Crush the Kasoori methi between your palms and stir it into the curry during the last 5 minutes of cooking.
Finish with a very small pinch of garam masala just before serving. Do not overuse it—the butter, tomatoes, and Kasoori methi should remain the dominant flavors.
Serve with garlic naan, butter naan, or jeera rice.
notes: Cooking the onions until deeply browned is one of the most important steps.
The gravy should be completely smooth before adding the butter, cream, and protein.
Do not skip the final one-hour simmer after the gravy is smooth—it dramatically improves the flavor.
Kasoori methi should always be added near the end to preserve its aroma.
Garam masala is only a finishing spice in authentic Delhi-style butter chicken.
For the best flavor, prepare the curry a day ahead and gently reheat before serving.
